item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So does the Star actually possess people who it reaches, or does it simply hollow them out and create a form of thinking exactly like itself?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He just- Christ, he was doing the same thing you're doing now.

vs

And then she starts in with this- this attitude about me being there

So is Shann a he or a she?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The feeling of not trusting what anyone says because you can't even trust yourself. It's something I know far too well…

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While Kaktus claims to have never read 1812

I don't like the implication here that I'm lying about this. Before last night, I'd never read this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I think the two pieces couldn’t be more different. 1812 is a horror piece predicated in natural and cosmic fears (cosmic being the idea of another hidden moon, and the natural being the fear of drowning)

This is effectively a character study. It’s a piece that is about the emotional and psychological aspects of feeling hated and disdained. It focuses itself entirely on that feeling and does so through character development.

Hell, 1812 has no dialogue while this piece is like… only dialogue.

I find that saying these too pieces are too similar is looking at it from a fairly denotative view. Sure, the two core anomalies might share some similarities, but my god the articles are almost the antithesis if each other in story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exceedingly similar to SCP-1812— both are astrological bodies with memetic effects that mean aren't visible unless they're pointed out, and both have potentially hazardous effects on the continued survival of those who view it. Where 1812 has physical effects such as phantom drowning, this has mental ones.

While Kaktus claims to have never read 1812, I see enough similarities here that I've downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well it does seem to exist in the nightsky. Buz since it is only visible to affected people…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I'm a little confused, is the star an actual physical star, or is it just a mind thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are certain elements of this that struck me as getting paranoia pretty spot-on (the stuff about sensing people's 'tone'; the desire to confide in others about your fears even as you fear they're 'in' on it too); the progression is good, as is the pacing. It's low key and restrained, which I like. I even like the repetition of the phrase, va va pi da (reminds me a bit of King's short, Lunch at the Gotham Cafe).

At the same time, I feel like the interviews could be… punchier? I'm not sure. Don't take this criticism too seriously, as I'm only half awake anyway — I just wanted to note that while I liked it enough to upvote, Saito's degeneration (and eventual shedding of his identity) didn't fill me with any particular sense of dread or foreboding. Even the gory implication felt more… expected? Than anything.

Maybe I'm just jaded with respect to SCPs — but part of me feels like there was a missed opportunity, somewhere. One where you could have dug deeper into the idea of an inescapable, twinkling star that inevitably leads you to hatred, paranoia, and self-loathing.

item.title: Oh…
item.content:encoded: Is Dr.Cato a Japanese?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fabulous example of something that does nothing but build upon itself through characterization. Easy +1, thanks for the read.

item.title: Amazing
item.content:encoded: I really liked…. well, everything about this SCP. I’m sure that a lot people can relate to these emotions as well. Definitely upvoting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow! Perhaps I'll give it shot myself eventually. Always thought it'd be cool to have my own signature formatting tweaks. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This hit very close to home in ways I'd rather not mention. Very bleak and disturbing in an esthetically satisfying way.

+1, hug your children if you have any.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great article acting as a vehicle for certain emotions and thought processes that are all too damn familiar. I think I'm starting to have ideas for something…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah that guy is nothing short of a miracle. We're beyond lucky to have him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep! You can tell because you can select the text. Before it was a series of images, which you couldn't select. This has a great benefit for the vision-impaired or those using screen readers, since they can now read the header and class text. Woedenaz is amazing for figuring out how to render it in HTML alone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
emulating my header images with a customizeable css module

Just curious, is that what you wound up using for this page?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An absolutely excellent piece. This is an article which really understands its characters and how to make things terrifying without being melodramatic. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good call, I saw the period inside the footnote and thought I was good.

Fix'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
some spag errors, in the collapsible below. Otherwise, it's pretty dang good. +1.

spelling and grammar
– hide block

Computerized voices are to be used to communicate with subjects in the later stages of affliction1

no period on this sentence.

"either" without an "or"

item.title: A Mean Celestial Body
item.content:encoded:
Love turns, with a little indulgence, to indifference or disgust;
hatred alone is immortal.

-William Hazlitt

A remix of an article that doesn't exist on this website anymore. I know we already sort of have one of those, but this is my go at it. I hope you like it!

Thanks to rounder, nico and woed for looking this over in chat, and a huge thanks to Woedenaz for ruining my productivity by emulating my header images with a customizeable css module. The dude really is a wizard.

Image credits are in the info bar.
